California
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornmission
July 13, 199o
Eonorable Kenneth L. Maddy
senate Republican Leader
state cap j,toI, Roon 305
Sacramento, CA 9 5814
*"' Iil'uli:"i:: f:;.:ll;'""' 
Advice
Dear Senator Maddy:
This is in response to your request for advice concerning
s/hether, under the Potitical Reform Act (the "Actrr),1 a
contributor can place Iirnitations on a candidate's use of his or
her contribution. 
Since your letter appears to present a
hypothetical question, $/e treat your reguest as one for informal
alii=t..rc" under neguiation 1832-9 (U) (e) io).2
OUESTTON
A candidate receives a contribution check which i-s
accompanied by the following statenent fron the contributor:
'tThi,s contribution can only be used for the campaign of the
rec j,pient. rt
Does this statement by the contributor prohibit the candj'date
from transferring al-I or a portion of the contribution to a ballot
measure committee controlled by the same candidate, controlled by
another candidate or not controlled by a candidate?
1 co.r".r,*"nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the covernment code unless other$rise indicated. 
commission
regulations appear at 2 California code of Regulations Section
18oOO, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 5 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informa] assistance does not provide the requestor with the
inmunity provided by an opinj.on or fornal written advj,ce. (Sectj.on
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 80O . P,O. Box 8O7 o Sacrarnento CA 958O4-O8OZ . (916)322-5660


Our File No. I-90-454
Page 2
CONCIJUSION
The Act permits a candidate who has made a particuLar
balfot measure an issue in his or her canpaign to transfer
his or her canpaign funds to a committee that supports or
opposes the baLLot measure, whether or not controlled by that
candidate, another candidate or no candidate at all. 
The
Commission staff vie\^/s this type of transfer as an
appropriate use of the candidite's contrj-butions for'seeking
political 
office. 
rn addition, the Act contains no
iequirernent that a candidate expend a contribution in a
manner prescribed by the contributor.
ANAI,,YSIS
The Act currently contains certain restrictions upon how
a candidate may expend campaign contributions. 
Section
852o2(b) states that contributions are "deemed to be held in
trustrt for expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the office he or she seeks or expenses
associated with holding that office. 
Section 85304 prohibits
a candidate from transferrinq contributions to other
candidates.3 Furthermore, Sections 85800 through 85807
(conmonly know as the rrpersonal use law") generally seek to
l-init the personaL benefit a candj-date can derive from
expending his or her carnpaign contributions.
Recently, the conmission determj-ned that candidates do
not violate Lhe transfer prohibition of Section 85304 in
using their carnpaj-gn funds to nake contributions to a ballot
measure committee controlled by another candidate (Hiltachk
Advice Letter, No. 89-533, dated March 16, L990, copy
enclosed.) The same letter stated that it was permissible
for a candidate to make similar transfers to ballot Eleasure
committees not controlled by another candidate. Implicit in
this analysis is the view that a candidate's expenditure of
campaign funds for this purpose is an expenditure associated
with the candidate's canpaign for office, as required by the
trust provisions in Section 85202 (b) . In the litilnan Advice
Letter, No. f-90-206 (copy enclosed), the Commission staff
further clarified the requirenents of the trust provj.sion,
stating that, to satisfy the provision, a candidate's
expenditure of campaign funds must be directly related to the
candidate's campaign for office, or be associated with
3 Section 85304 also prevents a candidate from using contrj,butions
received in running for one office to run for another office.
However, in the case of service Enplovees fnternational, Union, et
a1. v. FPPC (U.S. District court, Eastern District of California,
Case No. cM 89-0433 LKK-JFM) the court has issued a preliminary
injunction prohibiting enforcement of that provision.


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-454
Page 3
holding office. 
on the basis of these letters, we concLude that
r^rhere a candidate transfers contributions made to him or her to a
bal1ot measure comurittee, it is a perrnissible expenditure for the
purpose of seeking office as long as the candidate has made that
baIlot measure an issue in his or her campaign. consistent with
the Hiltachk letter, a transfer of this type is permitted
regardless of whether it is to a ballot measure committee
controlled by the candidate, another candidate or no candidate at
all.
Applying this analysis to your question, it is clear that,
since the type of transfer described above is deemed to be
associated with seeking office, it would be trfor the campaign of
the recipientrt and thus permissibl-e under the restriction placed
on the use of the contribution by your hypotheticaf contributor.
A larger question presented under the facts set forth in your
letter is whether a contributor can make a canpaign contrj,bution
to a candidate upon condition that the contribution be used for a
specific purpose. The Act sets forth only specif j,ed uses that nay
be made of campaign contributions. It does not prohibit the use
of contributions in a manner contrary to the expressed desire of a
contributor. 
Therefore, hrhen a candidate spends a contribution in
such a manner, that conduct al,one does not result in a violation
of the Act '
Final,Ly, pLease note that the commission only has
jurisdiction over areas of the law that are encompassed by the
Act. While a 1ega1 argunent could be rnade that a contribution
given under express conditj,ons is subject to contract Iaw or the
law of negotiable instruments, we have no jurisdiction over this
question and thus offer no opinion. our research has revealed no
California case faw on this point to which we can direct you.
I hope this reply has been of assistance. Should you have
additionaf questions, please contact ne at (916) 322-5901 .
S incer
